players.name: Mahmud Abunada
players.full_name: Mahmoud Ibrahim Mahmoud Abunada; محمود إبراهيم أبو ندى
players.bio: Young Doha-born stopper making his way through Qatar's ranks at Al-Rayyan. With just 5 caps, the 26-year-old is still writing his story — but a World Cup on home turf? That chapter writes itself.
players.facts: Born in Doha in 2000, Abunada is a product of Qatar's football revolution — the same era that built Aspire Academy.

players.name: Pedro Miguel
players.full_name: Pedro Miguel Carvalho Deus Correia; بيدرو ميجيل
players.bio: Born in Portugal but Qatari through and through, Pedro Miguel is closing in on 100 caps as a marauding right-back. Experienced, dependable, and always willing to bomb forward from Al-Sadd.
players.facts: Pedro Miguel is just one cap shy of a century for Qatar — he could hit 100 international appearances at the World Cup.

players.name: Lucas Mendes
players.bio: Brazilian-born centre-back who traded Curitiba for Qatar and never looked back. With 25 caps and a cultured left foot, Lucas Mendes brings South American grit to Al-Annabi's backline.
players.facts: Lucas Mendes was born in Curitiba — home of Brazil's famous Athletico Paranaense and some very passionate football fans.

players.name: Issa Laye
players.bio: A defensive option for Al-Arabi with 4 caps to his name, the 28-year-old Issa Laye is still establishing himself on the international stage. Solid and composed, he's eager to seize his moment.
players.facts: With only 4 caps heading into 2026, Laye could make his World Cup debut as one of Qatar's least-capped squad members.

players.name: Jassem Gaber
players.bio: Only 24 but already a seasoned figure with 32 caps, Jassem Gaber is one of Qatar's most exciting young defenders. The Al-Rayyan man combines composure with athleticism well beyond his years.
players.facts: Gaber was born in 2002 — the year Brazil won the World Cup. He now gets to play in one himself at just 24.

players.name: Abdulaziz Hatem
players.bio: A Qatar midfield institution with 117 caps, Abdulaziz Hatem has been pulling strings for over a decade. The Al-Rayyan veteran reads the game like a seasoned librarian — everything in its right place.
players.facts: Hatem has over 117 caps — one of the most capped players in Qatar's history, spanning more than a decade of service.

players.name: Ahmed Alaaeldin
players.bio: Pacy and direct, Ahmed Alaaeldin brings energy and goals to Qatar's attack. With 68 caps and 9 international goals, the Al-Rayyan forward is a trusted weapon for Al-Annabi in big moments.
players.facts: Alaaeldin has represented Qatar across two decades of his career, earning his first cap as a teenager back in the 2010s.

players.name: Edmilson Junior
players.bio: Naturalised forward who joined Qatar's ranks and added flair to their attack. Edmilson Junior brings Brazilian flair to Al-Duhail and Al-Annabi's frontline, though goals at international level have proven elusive.
players.facts: Edmilson Junior was born in Brazil — making him one of several Brazilian-born players who chose to represent Qatar internationally.

players.name: Mohammed Muntari
players.bio: Qatar's joint all-time top scorer with 16 goals in 67 caps, Muntari is a powerful, physical striker who never stops running. He famously scored Qatar's only goal at the 2022 World Cup on home soil.
players.facts: Muntari scored Qatar's sole goal at the 2022 World Cup — the only bright spot in a tough group stage for the hosts.

players.name: Hassan Al-Haydos
players.bio: Qatar's legendary captain with 186 caps and 41 goals, Al-Haydos is one of Asian football's true icons. Silky, creative, and seemingly ageless — he's been Qatar's number 10 for the best part of 15 years.
players.facts: With 186 caps, Al-Haydos is one of the most capped players in Asian football history. A living legend of Al-Annabi.

players.name: Akram Afif
players.bio: Qatar's crown jewel. The 2019 Asian Cup's best player, Afif is electric on the left wing — direct, quick, and utterly unplayable on his day. With 39 international goals, he's the man defenders fear most.
players.facts: Afif scored a hat-trick in the 2023 Asian Cup final — one of the greatest individual finals performances in Asian football history.

players.name: Karim Boudiaf
players.bio: Algerian-born powerhouse who became a cornerstone of Qatar's midfield. With 118 caps and a warrior's mentality, Karim Boudiaf disrupts, distributes, and drives — the engine Al-Annabi can't do without.
players.facts: Boudiaf was born in Algeria but has been a Qatar international since 2013, earning over 118 caps in over a decade of service.

players.name: Ayoub Al-Oui
players.bio: At just 21, Ayoub Al-Oui is one of the youngest players in Qatar's squad. The Al-Gharafa defender has 6 caps and enormous potential — the World Cup could be the making of this exciting prospect.
players.facts: Born in 2005, Al-Oui could be one of the youngest players to represent Qatar at a FIFA World Cup in the nation's history.

players.name: Homam Ahmed
players.bio: Unusually, Homam Ahmed plies his trade in Spain with Cultural Leonesa — a rare export from Qatar's scene. The versatile defender has 68 caps and brings European experience back to Al-Annabi's ranks.
players.facts: Homam Ahmed plays club football in Spain's lower leagues — a rare Qatari international with genuine European football experience.

players.name: Yusuf Abdurisag
players.bio: A lively forward with 39 caps for Qatar, Yusuf Abdurisag has developed into a useful option in Al-Annabi's attack. The Al-Wakrah man contributes both in wide areas and through the centre.
players.facts: Abdurisag has 3 international goals in 39 caps — a decent return for a wide forward often used in a supporting role.

players.name: Boualem Khoukhi
players.bio: Algerian-born Khoukhi is a barnstorming defender-midfielder hybrid with 116 caps and an incredible 20 international goals. A set-piece threat and a commanding presence — the complete defensive operator.
players.facts: Khoukhi has scored 20 international goals from defence — an astonishing tally that would be the envy of many strikers.

players.name: Ahmed Al-Ganehi
players.bio: A young midfielder on the rise, Ahmed Al-Ganehi has made 13 appearances for Qatar and notched his first international goal. The Al-Gharafa man is one to watch as he grows into his Al-Annabi role.
players.facts: Al-Ganehi was born in September 2000 — making him part of Qatar's exciting post-Aspire generation of homegrown talent.

players.name: Sultan Al-Brake
players.bio: A composed full-back or centre-back, Sultan Al-Brake brings solidity to Qatar's defensive unit. With 17 caps for the Al-Duhail man, he's earned his place in the squad through consistent domestic performances.
players.facts: Al-Brake's name is arguably the most memorable in Qatar's squad — and yes, defenders are supposed to apply the brakes.

players.name: Almoez Ali
players.bio: Qatar's all-time top scorer with 55 goals in 115 caps — Almoez Ali is simply a phenomenon. The Sudanese-born striker scored nine goals at the 2019 Asian Cup, breaking the tournament record.
players.facts: Almoez Ali broke the all-time Asian Cup scoring record with 9 goals in 2019 — surpassing a record held since 1996.

players.name: Ahmed Fathy
players.bio: A reliable midfield workhorse with 48 caps and zero international goals — suggesting Ahmed Fathy lets others do the glamour work. The Al-Arabi man is the selfless engine that keeps Qatar ticking.
players.facts: In 48 caps, Fathy has never scored for Qatar — proof that not every midfielder needs to be a goalscorer to earn their place.

players.name: Salah Zakaria
players.bio: Al-Duhail's young goalkeeper who has earned 8 caps for Qatar's national side. Salah Zakaria is pushing for a World Cup squad role and represents the next generation of Qatari goalkeeping talent.
players.facts: Zakaria was born in April 1999 — the same year Qatar first hosted the Gulf Cup, a tournament they'd go on to dominate.

players.name: Meshaal Barsham
players.bio: Valentine's Day baby and Qatar's number one stopper, Meshaal Barsham has 52 caps and is the undisputed first choice between the sticks. Commanding, agile, and born to keep clean sheets.
players.facts: Barsham was born on 14 February 1998 — Valentine's Day. Qatar fans certainly love him between the posts.

players.name: Assim Madibo
players.bio: A tireless holding midfielder, Assim Madibo has clocked 51 caps for Qatar. The Al-Wakrah man shields the defence, recycles possession, and does the thankless work that makes everyone else look better.
players.facts: In 51 caps, Madibo has never scored — his job is to stop goals, not score them, and he takes that responsibility seriously.

players.name: Tahsin Jamshid
players.bio: At just 19, Tahsin Jamshid is the jewel of Qatar's youth movement. The Al-Duhail teenager has 3 caps and could be playing a World Cup before he's old enough to rent a car in some countries.
players.facts: Born in June 2006, Jamshid could become one of the youngest players ever to appear for Qatar at a FIFA World Cup.

players.name: Al-Hashmi Al-Hussain
players.bio: Only 22, Al-Hashmi Al-Hussain is a promising young defender with 8 caps already under his belt. The Al-Arabi centre-back has time on his side and a World Cup stage to announce himself to the world.
players.facts: Born in August 2003, Al-Hussain turns 23 during the 2026 World Cup tournament — not a bad way to celebrate coming of age.

players.name: Mohamed Manai
players.bio: A 23-year-old forward from Al-Shamal, Mohamed Manai has 10 caps but is still chasing his first international goal. His pace and directness make him a useful squad option — and hungry to make his mark.
players.facts: Manai plays for Al-Shamal — one of the smaller clubs in Qatari football — making his international call-up a real rags-to-riches story.
